title: Deepening Hidden Representations from Pre-trained Language Models for Natural Language Understanding

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## DEEPENING HIDDEN REPRESENTATIONS FROM - PRE TRAINED LANGUAGE MODELS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Transformer-based pre-trained language models have proven to be effective for learning contextualized language representation. However, current approaches only take advantage of the output of the encoder’s final layer when fine-tuning the downstream tasks. We argue that only taking single layer’s output restricts the power of pre-trained representation. Thus we deepen the representation learned by the model by fusing the hidden representation in terms of an explicit HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE), which automatically absorbs the complementary representation with respect to the output from the final layer. Utilizing RoBERTa as the backbone encoder, our proposed improvement over the pre-trained models is shown effective on multiple natural language understanding tasks and help our model rival with the state-of-the-art models on the GLUE benchmark.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Language representation is essential to the understanding of text. Recently, pre-trained language models based on Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) such as GPT (Radford et al., 2018), BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLNet (Yang et al., 2019), and RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019c) have been shown to be effective for learning contextualized language representation. These models have since continued to achieve new state-of-the-art results on a variety of natural language processing tasks. They include question answering (Rajpurkar et al., 2018; Lai et al., 2017), natural language inference (Williams et al., 2018; Bowman et al., 2015), named entity recognition (Tjong Kim Sang & De Meulder, 2003), sentiment analysis (Socher et al., 2013) and semantic textual similarity (Cer et al., 2017; Dolan & Brockett, 2005).

Normally, Transformer-based models are pre-trained on large-scale unlabeled corpus in an unsupervised manner, and then fine-tuned on the downstream tasks through introducing task-specific output layer. When fine-tuning on the supervised downstream tasks, the models pass directly the output of Transformer encoder’s final layer, which is considered as the contextualized representation of input text, to the task-specific layer.

However, due to the numerous layers (i.e., Transformer blocks) and considerable depth of these pretrained models, we argue that the output of the last layer may not always be the best representation of the input text during the fine-tuning for downstream tasks. Devlin et al. (2019) shows diverse combinations of different layers’ outputs of the pre-trained BERT result in distinct performance on CoNLL-2003 Named Entity Recognition (NER) task (Tjong Kim Sang & De Meulder, 2003). Peters et al. (2018b) points out for pre-trained language models, including Transformer, the most transferable contextualized representations of input text tend to occur in the middle layers, while the top layers specialize for language modeling. Therefore, the onefold use of the last layer’s output may restrict the power of the pre-trained representation.

In this paper, we propose an extra network component design for Transformer-based model, which is capable of adaptively leveraging the hidden information in the Transformer’s hidden layers to refine the language representation. Our introduced additional components include two main additional components:
1. HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE) dynamically learns a complementary representation which contains the information that the final layer’s output fails to capture.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

2. Fusion network integrates the hidden information extracted by the HIRE with Transformer final layer’s output through two steps of functionalities, leading to a refined contextualized language representation.

Taking advantage of the robustness of RoBERTa by using it as our backbone Transformer-based encoder (Liu et al., 2019c), we conduct experiments on GLUE benchmark (Wang et al., 2018), which consists of nine Natural Language Understanding (NLU) tasks. With the help of HIRE, our model outperforms the baseline on 5/9 of them and advances the state-of-the-art on SST-2 dataset. Keeping the backbone Transformer model unchanged on its architecture, pre-training procedure and training objectives, we get comparable performance with other state-of-the-art models on the GLUE leaderboard, which verifies the effectiveness of the proposed HIRE enhancement over Transformer model.

## 2.1 TRANSFORMER-BASED ENCODER

Transformer-based encoder is responsible for encoding input text into contextualized representation. Let _{w_ 1 _, . . . , wn}_ represent a sequence of _n_ words of input text, Transformer-based encoder encodes the input sequence into its universal contextualized representation _**R** ∈_ R _[n][×][d]_ :

where _d_ is the hidden size of the encoder and _**R**_ is the output of Transformer-based encoder’s last layer which has the same length as the input text. We call it **preliminary representation** in this paper to distinguish it with the one that we introduce in section 2.2. Here, we omit a rather extensive formulations of Transformer and refer readers to Vaswani et al. (2017), Radford et al. (2018) and Devlin et al. (2019) for more details.

## 2.2 HIDDEN REPRESENTATION EXTRACTOR

Transformer-based encoder normally has many structure-identical layers stacked together, for example, BERTLARGE and XLNetLARGE all contain 24 layers of the identical structure, either outputs

from these hidden layers or the last layer, but not only limited to the latter, may be extremely helpful for specific downstream task. To make full use of the representations from these hidden layers, we introduce an extra component attached to the original encoder, HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE) to capture the complementary information that the output of the last layer fails to capture. Since each layer does not take the same importance to represent a certain input sequence for different downstream tasks, we design an adaptive mechanism that can compute the importance dynamically. We measure the importance by an **importance score** .

The input to the HIRE is _{_ _**H**_ 0 _, . . . ,_ _**H** j, . . . ,_ _**H** l}_ where _l_ represents the number of layers in the encoder. Here _**H**_ 0 is the initial embedding of input text, which is the input of the encoder’s first layer but is updated during training and _**H** j ∈_ R _[n][×][d]_ is the hidden-state of the encoder at the output of layer _j_ . For the sake of simplicity, we call them all hidden-state afterwards.

We use the same 2-layer Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) (Cho et al., 2014) to summarize each hidden-state of the encoder. Instead of taking the whole output of GRU as the representation of the hidden state, we concatenate GRU’s each layer and each direction’s final state together. With the same shape as the output of Transformer-based encoder’s final layer, HIRE’s output _**A**_ is expected to contain the additional useful information from the encoder’s hidden-states and we call it **complementary representation** .

## 2.3 FUSION NETWORK

This module fuses the information contained in the output of Transformer-based encoder and the one extracted from encoders’ hidden states by HIRE.

Given the preliminary representation _**R**_ , instead of letting it flow directly into task-specfic output layer, we combine it together with the complementary representation _**A**_ to yield _**M**_ , defined by:
where _◦_ is element-wise multiplication (Hadamard Product) and [; ] is concatenation across the last dimension.

Later, two-layer bidirectional GRU, with the output size of _d_ for each direction, is used to fully fuse the information contained in the preliminary representation and the complementary representation. ## 3 EXPERIMENTS

## 3.1 DATASET

We conducted the experiments on the General Language Understanding Evaluation (GLUE) benchmark (Wang et al., 2018) to evaluate our proposed method. GLUE is a collection of 9 diverse datasets[1] for training, evaluating, and analyzing natural language understanding models.

The results of MT-DNN, XLNETLARGE, ALBERT and RoBERTa are from Liu et al. (2019b), Yang et al. (2019), Lan et al. (2020) and Liu et al. (2019c). See the lower-most row for the performance of our approach. Mc, acc and pearson denote Matthews correlation, accuracy and Person correlation coefficient respectively.

Table 1 compares our method with a list of Transformer-based models on the development set. Model parameter comparison is shown in Table 2. To obtain a direct and fair comparison with our baseline model RoBERTa, following the original paper (Liu et al., 2019c), we fine-tune RoBERTa+HIRE separately for each of the GLUE tasks, using only task-specific training data. The single-model results for each task are reported. We run our model with five different random seeds but the same hyperparameters and take the median value. Due to the problematic nature of WNLI dataset, we exclude its results in this table. The results shows that RoBERTa+HIRE consistently outperforms RoBERTa on 4 of the GLUE task development sets, with an improvement of 1.7 points, 0.4 points, 0.5/0.2 points, 0.3 points on CoLA, SST-2, MNLI and QNLI respectively. And on the MRPC, STS-B and RTE task, our model get the same result as RoBERTa. It should be noted that the improvement is entirely attributed to the introduction of HIdden Representation Extractor and fusion network in our model.

Table 3: GLUE Test results, scored by the official evaluation server. All the results are obtained from GLUE leaderboard (https://gluebenchmark.com/leaderboard). (2019); _[b]_ Liu et al. (2019b); _[c]_ Zhu et al. (2020); _[d]_ Yang et al. (2019); _[e]_ Lan et al. (2020); _[f]_ Liu et al. (2019c).

Table 3 presents the results of HIRE enhancement and other models on the test set that have been submitted to the GLUE leaderboard. Following Liu et al. (2019c), we fine-tune STS-B and MRPC starting from the MNLI single-task model. Given the simplicity between RTE, WNLI and MNLI, and the large-scale nature of MNLI dataset (393k), we also initialize RoBERTa+HIRE with the weights of MNLI single-task model before fine-tuning on RTE and WNLI. We submitted the ensemble-model results to the leaderboard. The results show that RoBERTa+HIRE still boosts the strong RoBERTa baseline model on the test set. To be specific, RoBERTa+HIRE outperforms RoBERTa over CoLA, SST-2, MRPC, SST-B, MNLI-mm and QNLI with an improvement of 0.8 points, 0.4 points, 0.7/0.9 points, 0.2/0.1 points, 0.2 points and 0.1 points respectively. In the meantime, RoBERTa+HIRE gets the same results as RoBERTa on QQP and WNLI. By category, RoBERTa+HIRE has better performance than RoBERTa on the single sentence tasks, similarity and paraphrase tasks. It is worth noting that our model obtains state-of-the-art results on SST-2 dataset, with a score of 97.1. The results are quite promising since HIRE does not modify the encoder internal architecture (Yang et al., 2019) or redefine the pre-training procedure (Liu et al., 2019c) , getting the comparable results with them.

## 4 ABLATION STUDY

In this section, we perform a set of ablation experiments to understand the effects of our proposed techniques during fine-tuning. All the results reported in this section are a median of five random runs.

The results enable us to conclude that the hidden-state weighting mechanism introduced by HIRE may indeed adapt to diverse downstream tasks for better performance.

## 5 ANALYSIS

Figure 2: **Left** : Distribution of importance scores over different layers when computing the complementary representation for various NLU tasks. The numbers on the abscissa axis indicate the corresponding layer with 0 being the first layer and 24 being the last layer. **Right** : Distribution of importance scores over different layers for each example of SST-2 dataset. The number on the ordinate axis denotes the index of the example.

We compare the importance score’s distribution of different NLU tasks. For each task, we run our best single model over the development set and the results are calculated by averaging the values across all the examples within each dataset. The results are presented in the left of Figure 2. From the top to the bottom of the heatmap, the results are placed in the following order: single-sentence tasks, similarity and paraphrase tasks, and natural language inference tasks. From it, we find that the distribution differs among the different tasks, which demonstrates HIRE’s dynamic ability to adapt for distinct tasks when computing the complimentary representation. The most important contribution occurs below the final layer for all the tasks except STS-B and RTE. All layers have a close contribution for STS-B and RTE task. In addition, we find that QNLI, a classification task which requires the model to determine whether the context sentence contains the answer to the question, relies on layers 2-4 to some extent. In fact, Jawahar et al. (2019) state that BERT mostly captures phrase-level (or span-level) information in the lower layers and that this information gets gradually diluted in higher layers. Since QNLI is derived from the span extraction question answering dataset

SQuAD, where the answer is a span of the input text, we speculate that HIRE takes advantage of this information when making the prediction.

The right of Figure 2 presents the distribution of importance scores over different layers for each example of SST-2 dataset. The number on the ordinate axis denotes the index of the example. It shows that HIRE can adapt not only distinct tasks but also the different examples. In the meantime, we observe also that even though there are subtle differences among these examples, they follow certain same patterns when calculating the complementary representation, for example, layers 21 and 22 contribute the most for almost all the examples and also the layers around them. But the figure shows also that for some examples, all layers contribute nearly equally.

## 6 RELATED WORK

Transformer model is empowered by self-attention mechanism and has been applied as an effective model architecture design in quite a lot of pre-trained language models (Vaswani et al., 2017). OpenAI GPT (Radford et al., 2018) is the first model that introduced Transformer architecture into unsupervised pre-training. But instead of unidirectional training like GPT, BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) adopts Masked LM objective when pre-training, which enables the representation to incorporate context from both direction. The next sentence prediction (NSP) objective is also used by BERT to better model the relationship between sentences. Trained with dynamic masking, large mini-batches and a larger byte-level BPE, full-sentences without NSP, RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019c) improves BERT’s performance on the downstream tasks from a better BERT re-implementation training on BooksCorpus(Zhu et al., 2015), CC-News, Openwebtext and Stories. In terms of finetuning on downstream tasks, all these powerful Transformer-based models help various NLP tasks continuously achieve new state-of-the-art results. Diverse new methods have been proposed recently for fine-tuning the downstream tasks, including multi-task learning (Liu et al., 2019b), adversarial training (Zhu et al., 2020) or incorporating semantic information into language representation (Zhang et al., 2020).

Traditionally, downstream tasks or back-end part of models take representations from the last layer of the pre-trained language models as the default input. However, recent studies trigger researchers’ interest on the intermediate layers’ representation learned by pre-trained models. When studying the linguistic knowledge and transferability of contextualized word representations with a series of seventeen diverse probing tasks, Liu et al. (2019a) observe that Transformers tend to encode transferable features in their intermediate layers, aligned with the results by Peters et al. (2018b). Jawahar et al. (2019) show a rich hierarchy of linguistic information is encoded by BERT, with surface features, syntactic features and semantic features lying from bottom to top in the layers. So far, to our best knowledge, exploiting the representations learned by hidden layers is still stuck with limited empirical observations, which motivates us to propose a general solution for fully exploiting all levels of representations learned by the pre-trained language models. However, we want to note that a freezing/fine-tuning discrepancy may exist, since previous observations are made by adopting a feature extracting approach while our work is conducted in the fine-tuning scenario.

Several works have made attempts to utilize the information from the intermediate layers of deep models (Zhao et al., 2015; Peters et al., 2018a; Zhu et al., 2018; Tenney et al., 2019). Peters et al. (2018a) propose a deep contextualized word representation called ELMo, which is a task-specific linear combination of the intermediate layer representations in the bidirectional LSTM language model. Tenney et al. (2019) find that using ELMo-style scalar mixing of layer activations, both deep Transformer models (BERT and GPT) gain a significant performance improvement on a novel edge probing task. Zhu et al. (2018) adopt a linear combination of embeddings from different layers in BERT to encode tokens for conversational question answering but in a feature-based way. Compared with these works, our approach still presents the following innovations: 1) Our dynamic layer weighting mechanism calculates importance scores for each example over different layers, which is distinct from previous works (e.g., ELMo), where the contribution score (in the form of a parameter) for each layer is always same for all the examples of the same task. 2) Our approach fuses the representation weighted from the hidden layers and the one from the last layer to get a refined representation. 3) To our best knowledge, our work is the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model’s intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.

## 7 CONCLUSION

This paper presents HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE), a novel enhancement component that refines language representation by adaptively leveraging the Transformer-based model’s hidden layers. In our proposed model design, HIRE dynamically generates complementary representation from all hidden layers other than that from the default last layer. A lite fusion network then incorporates the outputs of HIRE into those of the original model. The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of refined language representation for natural language understanding. The analysis highlights the distinct contribution of each layer’s output for diverse tasks and different examples.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a conceptually interesting extension to pre-trained language models by explicitly fusing intermediate transformer layers via a learnable mechanism (HIRE). The motivation—leveraging complementary information from hidden layers—is well-aligned with recent findings in probing studies. The model achieves modest but consistent gains on GLUE, particularly on CoLA and SST-2, and provides interpretable attention-like weights over layers, which adds diagnostic value.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient comparison to simple fusion baselines: The paper fails to compare HIRE against straightforward, parameter-efficient alternatives such as learned or fixed averaging of hidden layers (e.g., ELMo-style scalar mixing or uniform pooling). The ablation in Table 5 only compares dynamic weighting to mean/random over subsets of layers, but does not test whether a simple learned linear combination of all hidden states—without Bi-GRU summarization or complex fusion—could achieve similar gains. Given that recent work shows such baselines perform competitively (e.g., 'BERT Layers Combination Yields Better Sentence Embeddings', 2023), the added complexity of HIRE is not justified. Readers will question whether the improvements stem from architectural design or merely from introducing additional capacity. The authors should include a direct comparison to a linear layer mixing all hidden states as a strong baseline.

- Fusion network design introduces redundancy and unclear contribution: Equation (6) concatenates R, A, R+A, and R∘A—four terms that are not independent. The element-wise product and sum are already expressible via affine transformations of the concatenation [R; A], making the expanded input M ∈ ℝ^(n×4d) potentially redundant. This raises concerns about whether the Bi-GRU in Equation (7) is learning meaningful interactions or simply overfitting to noise. The ablation study removes the entire fusion network but does not isolate the contribution of each term in M. A more rigorous analysis would test simplified versions—e.g., using only [R; A]—to determine which components are necessary. Without this, the fusion strategy appears unnecessarily complex and risks overfitting, especially given the small size of tasks like CoLA (8.5k examples).

- Overfitting risk due to parameter inflation not addressed: Table 2 shows RoBERTa+HIRE increases parameters from 355M to 437M—an 82M increase—primarily from the Bi-GRUs and fusion components. However, the paper does not report any regularization strategies beyond standard fine-tuning (e.g., dropout, weight decay), nor does it analyze whether the gains diminish with smaller data. Tasks like RTE (2.5k examples) and WNLI (634 examples) show no improvement, which may indicate overfitting. The authors claim the improvement is due to HIRE, but without controlled experiments on low-resource settings or comparisons to regularized variants, it is unclear whether the added parameters are justified. A parameter efficiency analysis (e.g., performance vs. FLOPs or parameter count) would strengthen the contribution.

- Lack of direct comparison to contemporaneous hidden-layer fusion methods: The paper positions HIRE as the first method to leverage intermediate layers in fine-tuning, but this claim is incorrect. Works like ELMo (2018), StructBERT (2019), and more recently HyPe (ACL 2023) and MoLEx (ICLR 2025) explicitly use or perturb hidden representations during fine-tuning. The related work section acknowledges ELMo but downplays its relevance by noting it uses fixed weights per task—yet HIRE also learns task-specific parameters (via W and b in Equation 3), so the distinction is blurred. The absence of quantitative comparisons to these methods, especially HyPe or MoLEx which target similar goals, undermines the novelty claim. The authors should either revise the claim or include a comparison to at least one such method under the same fine-tuning protocol.

- Interpretation of learned weights conflates correlation with utility: Figure 2 presents the distribution of learned importance scores across layers and claims this shows HIRE adaptively selects useful representations. However, the scores are derived from a Bi-GRU summary and a linear projection (Equation 3), which may reflect model dynamics rather than true layer utility. Without intervention experiments—e.g., ablating high-weight layers or comparing to gradient-based attribution—the analysis remains descriptive. For example, high weights on layers 21–22 in SST-2 could reflect residual influence rather than semantic contribution. A more rigorous analysis would test whether freezing or masking high-scoring layers degrades performance more than low-scoring ones.

- No comparison to ELMo-style linear layer mixing: The paper claims HIRE’s dynamic weighting improves performance over single-layer use, but does not test whether simpler, well-established alternatives like learned linear combinations of all hidden states (e.g., ELMo-style scalar mixing) could achieve similar gains. This is a standard baseline in the literature for layer fusion, and omitting it leaves open the possibility that the improvement comes from increased model capacity rather than the specific design of HIRE. The authors should implement a variant of RoBERTa+HIRE that replaces the Bi-GRU summarization and dynamic scoring with a learned scalar mixture of all 25 layers—i.e., compute $  m{A} =  rac{1}{Z} 	ext{softmax}( m{ eta})  m{H} $, where $  m{ eta} 
eq  m{ eta}_{	ext{task}} $ is a single learned vector—and compare its performance on CoLA and SST-2 to the full model. Without this, the necessity of the full HIRE architecture remains unproven.

- Fusion input redundancy not validated: The fusion network constructs $  m{M} $ using four terms: $  m{R},  m{A},  m{R}+ m{A},  m{R}	imes m{A} $. Since $  m{R}+ m{A} $ and $  m{R}	imes m{A} $ are functions of the first two, this representation is redundant and may inflate dimensionality without benefit. The ablation study removes the entire fusion network but does not isolate which components are useful. A minimal test would be to replace $  m{M} $ with just $ [ m{R};  m{A}] $ and retrain on CoLA and STS-B. If performance drops are minor, it would suggest the full design is needlessly complex. The paper should include such a control to justify the architectural choice and rule out overfitting due to high-dimensional inputs.

- No analysis of performance vs. data size: The model adds significant parameters (355M → 437M) and shows no improvement on low-data tasks like RTE (2.5k) or WNLI (634). This raises concerns about overfitting in small-data regimes. To demonstrate robustness, the authors should run a controlled experiment: subsample SST-2 (67k) to 5k and 1k examples and compare RoBERTa vs. RoBERTa+HIRE. If HIRE fails to improve or even degrades performance at lower data sizes, it would indicate limited generalizability. Such an analysis is standard in NLP and necessary to support claims of broad effectiveness across GLUE tasks.

- Missing comparison to StructBERT or HyPe: The paper claims to be the first to leverage intermediate layers in fine-tuning, but StructBERT (2019) and HyPe (ACL 2023) both explicitly modify or access hidden representations during fine-tuning. While related work mentions ELMo, it does not engage with these more relevant contemporaneous methods. A direct comparison on the same fine-tuning setup—e.g., reporting HyPe’s results on SST-2 or QNLI under single-task tuning—would clarify the novelty and relative benefit of HIRE. At minimum, the authors should re-evaluate their claim in light of StructBERT’s use of layer reinitialization and auxiliary objectives on hidden states.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add a direct comparison to a strong baseline: a learned linear combination of all hidden states (ELMo-style mixing) without Bi-GRU summarization.

- Simplify and ablate the fusion network design, testing whether concatenation [R; A] alone suffices versus the current four-term construction.

- Include a parameter efficiency or low-data analysis to rule out overfitting as the source of gains, especially for small datasets like RTE and CoLA.

- Revise claims of novelty and add comparison to at least one contemporaneous method that uses intermediate layers in fine-tuning (e.g., HyPe or MoLEx).

- Strengthen the analysis of learned weights with causal or ablation-based evidence linking high scores to functional importance.

comments.title: Index out of bounds in hidden state summation
comments.quote: \bm{A}=\sum_{i=0}^{l+1}s_{i}\bm{H}_{i}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d}$ (5)
comments.explanation: The summation in Equation (5) runs from $i=0$ to $l+1$, but the hidden states are defined only up to index $l$. This implies the existence of $\bm{H}_{l+1}$, which is undefined. Since $\bm{s} \in \mathbb{R}^{l+1}$ from the softmax over $l+1$ elements, the upper bound must be $l$. The current formulation introduces a formal inconsistency. Correct the summation to $\sum_{i=0}^{l}$ to align with the defined set $\{\bm{H}_0, \dots, \bm{H}_l\}$.

comments.title: Unjustified ReLU in unnormalized importance scores
comments.quote: \alpha_{i}=\text{ReLU}(\bm{W}^{T}\bm{u}_{i}+b)\in\mathbb{R}$ (3)
comments.explanation: Applying ReLU to the unnormalized scores $\alpha_i$ forces them to be non-negative before softmax normalization. This is unnecessary and potentially harmful: standard attention mechanisms use raw linear projections, allowing the model to assign small but non-zero weights even when $\bm{W}^T\bm{u}_i + b$ is negative. The ReLU introduces an unanalyzed sparsity bias, which may zero out useful layers. Since no ablation justifies this choice, remove the ReLU and use $\alpha_i = \bm{W}^T\bm{u}_i + b$ to maintain consistency with standard practice.

comments.title: Redundant and inflated input in fusion network
comments.quote: \bm{M}=[\bm{R};\bm{A};\bm{R}+\bm{A};\bm{R}\circ\bm{A}]\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times 4d}$ (6)
comments.explanation: The terms $\bm{R}+\bm{A}$ and $\bm{R}\circ\bm{A}$ are deterministic functions of $\bm{R}$ and $\bm{A}$. A Bi-GRU or MLP with sufficient capacity can learn such interactions implicitly. Including them explicitly inflates the input dimension from $2d$ to $4d$ without justification. This increases model complexity and risks overfitting, especially on low-data tasks. The ablation study removes the entire fusion network but does not test whether simpler input constructions (e.g., $[\bm{R}; \bm{A}]$) suffice. Add an ablation comparing $\bm{M}$ variants to validate the necessity of expanded features.

comments.title: Invalid ablation design undermines fusion network evaluation
comments.quote: - We remove the fusion network, and take the outputs of HIRE as the final representation of the input and flow it directly into the output layer and present the results in row 3.
comments.explanation: When the fusion network is removed, the output of HIRE (a $d$-dimensional dynamic weighted sum) is fed directly into the output layer, which expects a $2d$-dimensional input (from $\bm{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 2d}$). This creates a dimensionality mismatch unless an implicit projection is applied. The reported 1.5-point drop on CoLA may reflect this architectural incompatibility rather than the value of the fusion network. To isolate the fusion component, add a linear projection from $d$ to $2d$ when the fusion network is removed, or rephrase the claim to acknowledge that the 'fusion network' includes both interaction modeling and dimension matching.

comments.title: Factual error in novelty claim about fine-tuning usage
comments.quote: To our best knowledge, our work is the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model’s intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: This claim is incorrect. StructBERT (2019) applies auxiliary objectives to hidden layers during fine-tuning, and HyPe (ACL 2023) perturbs hidden representations in fine-tuning for robustness. MoLEx (ICLR 2025) also modifies hidden routing during fine-tuning. While HIRE introduces a distinct mechanism, it is not the first to leverage intermediate layers in the fine-tuning phase. The claim should be revised to acknowledge these works and position HIRE as a novel approach rather than a first-of-its-kind.

comments.title: Misleading interpretation of 'random' weighting baseline
comments.quote: random, all 25 layers | 69.3 | (-0.4)
comments.explanation: The 'random' weighting strategy is described as generating scores per example and applying softmax, but this is not a fixed or static baseline—it still allows instance-specific weighting, just without learning. The comparison is therefore not valid for claiming that 'adaptive assignment' is necessary, since both HIRE and the 'random' condition are dynamic. If the goal is to test learned vs. fixed weights, the random scores should be shared across all examples. Otherwise, relabel this condition as 'non-learned dynamic' and clarify that the comparison evaluates learning, not adaptivity per se.

comments.title: Overinterpretation of layer importance patterns in Figure 2
comments.quote: layers 21 and 22 contribute the most for almost all the examples and also the layers around them.
comments.explanation: The right side of Figure 2 shows high variability in importance scores across examples, with no consistent peak at layers 21–22. The heatmap does not support the claim that these layers dominate across 'almost all' examples. Some show high weights there, but many do not. This appears to be an overinterpretation of noise or minor trends. Remove the generalization or back it with quantitative analysis, such as mean importance per layer across examples, to avoid suggesting false consistency.

comments.title: Incorrect citation for layer utility in BERT
comments.quote: *Devlin et al. (2019)* shows diverse combinations of different layers’ outputs of the pre-trained BERT result in distinct performance on CoNLL-2003 Named Entity Recognition (NER) task
comments.explanation: Devlin et al. (2019) introduced BERT but did not explore layer combinations on NER or any downstream task. They used only the final layer representation. The finding that different layer combinations yield varying performance was demonstrated later, e.g., in Liu et al. (2019a) or Tenney et al. (2019). This misattribution weakens the paper's motivation. Cite a study that actually performed layer ablation or fusion experiments on BERT, such as Tenney et al. (2019) on probing or Liu et al. (2019a) on RoBERTa layer usage.

comments.title: Inconsistent dimensionality in output layer description
comments.quote: take the first row  $c \in \mathbb{R}^{2d}$  of  $\pmb{F}$  corresponding to the first input token  $(< s >)$  as the aggregated representation.
comments.explanation: The representation $c$ is said to be in $\mathbb{R}^{2d}$, but RoBERTa's hidden states are $d$-dimensional. The factor of 2 arises only after fusion in $\bm{F} = \text{Bi-GRU}(\bm{M}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 2d}$. However, this section does not define $\pmb{F}$, making it seem as if the base model outputs $2d$-dimensional vectors. This is misleading. Either redefine $\pmb{F}$ in this section or clarify that it refers to the fused representation from prior components.

comments.title: Incorrect token notation for RoBERTa input
comments.quote: corresponding to the first input token  $(< s >)$
comments.explanation: The notation `(< s >)` is non-standard and ambiguous. RoBERTa uses `<s>` as the beginning-of-sentence token in fairseq-style BPE. The spacing and parentheses suggest a typesetting error. This could confuse readers about whether it refers to `<s>`, `[CLS]`, or another special token. Rewrite as `<s>` without extra spacing or parentheses to match standard tokenizer conventions.

comments.title: Spelling error: 'complimentary' vs. 'complementary'
comments.quote: when computing the complimentary representation
comments.explanation: The term 'complimentary' is used incorrectly. The intended word is 'complementary', meaning mutually enhancing components. 'Complimentary' means expressing praise or given free of charge. This error occurs multiple times and undermines precision. Replace all instances of 'complimentary representation' with 'complementary representation'.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes HIRE, a method to fuse representations from multiple Transformer layers, showing modest improvements on GLUE tasks. While the approach is sensible and well-motivated, the evaluation has significant gaps regarding statistical significance, computational efficiency, and comparison with related multi-layer fusion methods.

The paper presents a well-motivated approach for leveraging intermediate Transformer representations through dynamic layer weighting and fusion. The ablation studies are reasonably comprehensive, and the GLUE results show consistent but small improvements. However, the evaluation lacks statistical significance testing, computational analysis, and comparison with recent multi-layer fusion methods, making the contribution's practical impact unclear.

Overview issues:

- Missing Statistical Significance Testing: The paper reports performance improvements as median values over 5 runs but provides no statistical significance testing. Given that most improvements are less than 1 point (e.g., +0.4 on SST-2, +0.3 on QNLI), readers cannot determine whether these gains are meaningful or within noise variance. For Table 1's CoLA improvement of +1.7 points, we need confidence intervals or p-values to assess significance. The absence of statistical testing is particularly problematic for small improvements that could be due to random initialization or training variance.

- Incomplete Computational Cost Analysis: Table 2 shows parameter count increases from 355M to 437M (23% increase), but the paper provides no analysis of training time, inference latency, or memory overhead. For practical deployment, readers need to know whether the 0.2-0.5 point GLUE improvements justify the computational costs. The paper should report training time per epoch, inference speed (examples/second), and peak memory usage compared to RoBERTa baseline. Without this analysis, the method's practical utility remains unclear.

- Insufficient Comparison with Multi-layer Fusion Methods: The related work mentions ELMo-style layer mixing but lacks comparison with recent multi-layer fusion approaches like Depth-Wise Attention (DWAtt, LREC 2024) or other attention-based fusion methods. The paper only compares against single-layer baselines (RoBERTa, XLNet) rather than other multi-layer fusion techniques. This omission limits understanding of HIRE's relative performance and novelty. The authors should include comparisons with at least 2-3 recent multi-layer fusion methods on GLUE tasks.

- Unvalidated Complementarity Assumption: The paper assumes hidden layers contain complementary information but provides no direct evidence. Figure 2's heatmap shows importance scores but doesn't demonstrate that extracted representations are actually complementary to the final layer. A probing analysis or visualization showing what information HIRE captures that the final layer misses would strengthen this claim. For example, the authors could analyze whether HIRE better captures syntactic information that higher layers lose, as suggested by Jawahar et al. (2019).

- Limited Dynamic vs Static Comparison: Table 5 compares the dynamic mechanism with mean and random strategies but doesn't compare with learned static weights (like ELMo's scalar mixing). This is a more meaningful baseline since ELMo demonstrates that task-specific static weights can be effective. The paper should include a comparison with ELMo-style learned static weights to properly evaluate whether the dynamic mechanism provides additional benefits beyond simpler approaches.

- Lack of Architecture Generalization: The method is only evaluated on RoBERTa, leaving unanswered whether HIRE works equally well with other architectures like BERT, XLNet, or encoder-decoder models. The paper's claim of being a general enhancement for Transformer-based models requires validation across architectures. The authors should at least test HIRE with BERT-base to demonstrate architecture independence.

- Missing demonstration of complementarity: The paper claims HIRE extracts complementary information that the final layer fails to capture, but provides no direct evidence. Figure 2 shows importance scores but doesn't demonstrate that the extracted representations actually contain information missing from the final layer. A probing analysis comparing what linguistic features (syntactic, semantic) are captured by HIRE versus the final layer would validate this core assumption. For example, the authors could use edge probing tasks to show HIRE better preserves syntactic information that higher layers lose, as suggested by Jawahar et al. (2019).

- No worked example of fusion mechanism: The paper presents the fusion network equations (6-7) but never walks through a concrete computation. Readers would benefit from a worked example showing how the fusion operates on actual hidden states. The authors could take a sample sentence from SST-2, show the preliminary representation R from RoBERTa's last layer, the complementary representation A computed by HIRE, and step through the concatenation and Bi-GRU fusion to produce the final representation F. This would clarify how the different fusion operations (concatenation, addition, element-wise product) contribute to the refined representation.

- Missing implementation guidance for dynamic mechanism: The dynamic importance scoring mechanism (equations 3-4) is presented theoretically but without practical implementation details. Practitioners need guidance on hyperparameter settings for the ReLU activation, weight initialization, and potential numerical stability issues. The paper should include implementation tips such as: initialization strategy for W and b, whether batch normalization helps stabilize the softmax, and how to handle the case where all αᵢ become negative after ReLU. This practical guidance is essential for reproducibility and adoption.

- Unverified generalization beyond RoBERTa: The paper positions HIRE as a general enhancement for Transformer-based models but only evaluates it with RoBERTa. To substantiate this claim, the authors need at least one additional architecture validation. Testing HIRE with BERT-base would demonstrate that the method works beyond RoBERTa's specific training regime. The paper should report GLUE results for BERT-base+HIRE compared to BERT-base baseline, showing whether similar improvements are achieved. This validation is necessary given the paper's broad claims about enhancing all Transformer-based models.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add statistical significance testing with confidence intervals or p-values for all reported improvements

- Include comprehensive computational analysis showing training time, inference speed, and memory overhead compared to RoBERTa baseline

- Add comparisons with recent multi-layer fusion methods (DWAtt, ELMo variants, etc.) on GLUE tasks

- Provide probing analysis or evidence that HIRE extracts complementary information not present in final layer

- Test HIRE on at least one additional Transformer architecture (e.g., BERT) to demonstrate generalization

- Add comparison with ELMo-style static weight learning as a more meaningful baseline for dynamic mechanism

comments.title: Index range inconsistency in summation
comments.quote:
Subsequently, we obtain the input sequence’s new representation $\bm{A}$ by:

$\bm{A}=\sum_{i=0}^{l+1}s_{i}\bm{H}_{i}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d}$ (5)
comments.explanation: The summation index in equation (5) is inconsistent with the defined range of hidden states. The input to HIRE is defined as $\{\bm{H}_{0},\dots,\bm{H}_{j},\dots,\bm{H}_{l}\}$ where $l$ represents the number of layers, giving us $l+1$ hidden states indexed from 0 to $l$. However, equation (5) sums from $i=0$ to $l+1$, which would require $\bm{H}_{l+1}$ that doesn't exist. This creates a mathematical error since $s_{l+1}$ and $\bm{H}_{l+1}$ are undefined. The correct summation should be from $i=0$ to $l$ to match the available hidden states. Rewrite equation (5) as $\bm{A}=\sum_{i=0}^{l}s_{i}\bm{H}_{i}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d}$ because the summation range must match the defined hidden state indices.

comments.title: ReLU output violates softmax input requirements
comments.quote:
Then the importance value $\alpha_{i}$ for hidden-state $\bm{H}_{i}$ is calculated by:

$\alpha_{i}=\text{ReLU}(\bm{W}^{T}\bm{u}_{i}+b)\in\mathbb{R}$ (3)

where $\bm{W}\in\mathbb{R}^{4d\times 1}$ and $b\in\mathbb{R}$ are trainable parameters. Let $\bm{\alpha}$=$\{\alpha_{i}\}$ be normalized into a probability distribution $\bm{s}$ through a softmax layer:

$\bm{s}=\text{softmax}(\bm{\alpha})\in\mathbb{R}^{l+1}$ (4)
comments.explanation: The combination of ReLU and softmax in equations (3)-(4) creates a mathematical inconsistency. ReLU outputs are non-negative ($\alpha_{i} \geq 0$), and softmax is defined as $\text{softmax}(\bm{x})_i = \frac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_j e^{x_j}}$. When all $\alpha_{i} \geq 0$, the softmax becomes $s_i = \frac{e^{\alpha_i}}{\sum_j e^{\alpha_j}}$, which is mathematically valid but loses the intended normalization property. More critically, if all $\alpha_i = 0$ (which can happen when $\bm{W}^{T}\bm{u}_{i}+b \leq 0$ for all i), the softmax becomes undefined due to division by zero in the denominator. The authors should either remove the ReLU and use the raw linear scores, or add a small epsilon to prevent numerical instability. Replace equation (3) with $\alpha_{i}=\bm{W}^{T}\bm{u}_{i}+b\in\mathbb{R}$ to ensure stable softmax computation.

comments.title: Bi-GRU output dimension mismatch
comments.quote:
We use the same 2-layer Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) *(Cho et al., 2014)* to summarize each hidden-state of the encoder. Instead of taking the whole output of GRU as the representation of the hidden state, we concatenate GRU’s each layer and each direction’s final state together. In this way, we manage to summarize the hidden-state into a fixed-sized vector. Hence, we obtain $\bm{U}\in\mathbb{R}^{(l+1)\times 4d}$ with $\bm{u}_{i}$ the summarized vector of $\bm{H}_{i}$:

$\bm{u}_{i}=\text{Bi-GRU}(\bm{H}_{i})\in\mathbb{R}^{4d}$ (2)
comments.explanation: There's a dimension mismatch in the Bi-GRU processing. Each $\bm{H}_{i} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ represents hidden states for $n$ tokens with $d$ dimensions. A 2-layer bidirectional GRU processes sequences and outputs $\mathbb{R}^{n \times 2d}$ (concatenated forward and backward hidden states). However, the paper claims to extract only the final states from each layer and direction, which would be 4 vectors of size $d$ each (2 layers × 2 directions), giving $4d$ dimensions total. This is inconsistent with standard GRU behavior where final states are typically $d$-dimensional vectors, not $n \times d$ matrices. The notation $\text{Bi-GRU}(\bm{H}_{i})$ is ambiguous - it should clarify whether it's extracting final states or processing the full sequence. Replace equation (2) with $\bm{u}_{i}=\text{concat}(\bm{h}^{(1)}_{\text{final, fwd}}, \bm{h}^{(1)}_{\text{final, bwd}}, \bm{h}^{(2)}_{\text{final, fwd}}, \bm{h}^{(2)}_{\text{final, bwd}}) \in \mathbb{R}^{4d}$ to accurately describe the concatenation of final hidden states.

comments.title: Ambiguous regression task implementation
comments.quote: For regression task, we obtain  $q$  in the same manner with  $m = 1$ , and take  $q$  as the predicted value.
comments.explanation: The regression implementation is unclear. Setting $m = 1$ in equation (8) gives a scalar output $q$, but the equation includes a softmax operation in (9) which is inappropriate for regression tasks. The authors should specify that for regression, they skip the softmax step and directly use the scalar $q$ as the prediction. Alternatively, they could present a separate regression equation without the softmax. As written, it suggests they apply softmax to a single value, which would always output 1.0.

comments.title: Incorrect ablation calculation for fusion network
comments.quote: We also observe that fusion network is an important component that contributes  $1.5 / 0.2$  gains on the CoLA and STS-B tasks.
comments.explanation: The reported gains of 1.5/0.2 points for the fusion network are mathematically incorrect based on the table data. For CoLA: 69.7 (Our model) - 68.2 (w/o Fusion) = 1.5 points ✓. For STS-B: 92.4 (Our model) - 92.2 (w/o Fusion) = 0.2 points ✓. However, the calculation method is flawed because it doesn't account for the compounding effect of removing HIRE. The proper way to isolate the fusion network's contribution would be to compare (Our model with HIRE but w/o Fusion) against (Our model with HIRE and Fusion), not against the baseline that also removes HIRE. Rewrite 'We also observe that fusion network is an important component that contributes  $1.5 / 0.2$  gains on the CoLA and STS-B tasks' as 'We also observe that fusion network contributes 1.5 and 0.2 points on CoLA and STS-B respectively when comparing our full model against the version without fusion network' because the current calculation conflates the effects of removing both components.

comments.title: Table 4 missing STS-B results column
comments.quote:
Table 4: Ablation study over model design consideration on the development set of CoLA and STS-B. The result for each model is a median of five random runs. Both HIRE and fusion network significantly improve the model performance on all two datasets.

| Method | Mc | |
| --- | --- | --- |
| HIRE (dynamic) | 69.7 | |
comments.explanation: Table 4 claims to show results for both CoLA and STS-B datasets, but only displays one column labeled 'Mc' (Matthews correlation) with no STS-B results. The table header promises results for two datasets but only provides CoLA metrics. This creates an inconsistency between the table description and its actual content. The table should either include a second column for STS-B results (likely Pearson/Spearman correlation) or the caption should be corrected to indicate only CoLA results are shown.

comments.title: Table 5 missing method labels
comments.quote:
Table 5: Effect of dynamic mechanism when computing the importance scores. A median Matthews correlation of five random runs is reported for CoLA on the development set.

We investigate how the adaptive assignment mechanism of the importance score affects the model's performance. CoLA is chosen as the downstream task. Table 5 compares our proposed mechanism

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

| Number | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Mc | 68.5 | 69.3 | 69.7 | 68.5 |
comments.explanation: Table 5 is presented with numeric column headers (0, 1, 2, 3) but no explanation of what these numbers represent. The text mentions comparing the proposed mechanism with 'diverse fixed combinations' but doesn't map these to the table columns. Readers cannot determine which method corresponds to each performance value. The table should include clear method labels (e.g., 'mean-all', 'random-last6', etc.) instead of ambiguous numbers.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about fine-tuning novelty
comments.quote: 3) To our best knowledge, our work is the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model’s intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: This claim is factually incorrect. Several prior works have utilized intermediate layer information during fine-tuning scenarios. For example, Tenney et al. (2019) demonstrated ELMo-style scalar mixing of BERT and GPT layer activations during fine-tuning on edge probing tasks. Additionally, Zhang et al. (2020) 'Utilizing BERT Intermediate Layers for Aspect Based Sentiment Analysis' explicitly fine-tuned BERT while incorporating intermediate layer representations for sentiment analysis. The authors should correct this claim to accurately reflect that their contribution is the dynamic weighting mechanism rather than being the first to use intermediate layers in fine-tuning.

comments.title: Inconsistent performance claim with abstract
comments.quote: With the help of HIRE, our model outperforms the baseline on 5/9 of them and advances the state-of-the-art on SST-2 dataset.
comments.explanation: This statement contradicts the abstract's claim that the model 'help our model with the state-of-the-art models on the GLUE benchmark.' The introduction only mentions advancing SOTA on SST-2 and outperforming baseline on 5/9 tasks, which is more modest than the abstract's suggestion of competing with SOTA models across GLUE. The authors should align these claims - either the introduction should match the abstract's broader claim, or the abstract should be revised to reflect the more specific 5/9 baseline improvements mentioned here.

comments.title: Inconsistent dimension specification in Bi-GRU output
comments.quote:
ter, two-layer bidirectional GRU, with the output size of $d$ for each direction, is used to fully fuse the information contained in the preliminary representation and the complementary representation. We concatenate the outputs of the GRUs in two dimensions together for the final contextualized representation:

$\bm{F}=\text{Bi-GRU}(\bm{M})\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times 2d}$ (7)
comments.explanation:
The text states the Bi-GRU has output size $d$ for each direction, but the phrase 'concatenate the outputs of the GRUs in two dimensions together' is ambiguous. For a two-layer bidirectional GRU with hidden size $d$ per direction, if we concatenate forward and backward outputs from the final layer, the dimension is indeed $\mathbb{R}^{n\times 2d}$. However, if we concatenate across both layers and directions, it would be $\mathbb{R}^{n\times 4d}$. The current description doesn't specify which concatenation is performed.

Rewrite the description as: 'Later, a two-layer bidirectional GRU with hidden size $d$ for each direction processes $\bm{M}$. The forward and backward outputs from the final layer are concatenated to produce the final contextualized representation: $\bm{F}=\text{Bi-GRU}(\bm{M})\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times 2d}$' because this clarifies that only the final layer's bidirectional outputs are concatenated.

comments.title: Overstated generalization claim
comments.quote: This paper presents HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE), a novel enhancement component that refines language representation by adaptively leveraging the Transformer-based model’s hidden layers. In our proposed model design, HIRE dynamically generates complementary representation from all hidden layers other than that from the default last layer. A lite fusion network then incorporates the outputs of HIRE into those of the original model. The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of refined language representation for natural language understanding. The analysis highlights the distinct contribution of each layer’s output for diverse tasks and different examples.
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims HIRE works for 'Transformer-based model's hidden layers' in general, but the paper only evaluates HIRE with RoBERTa according to the abstract and results sections. This overgeneralization is not supported by the experimental evidence. The paper should limit claims to RoBERTa or provide evidence of generalization across architectures.

comments.title: Inconsistent token notation in classification task
comments.quote: For classification task, given the input text's contextualized representation  $\pmb{F}$ , following Devlin et al. (2019), we take the first row  $c \in \mathbb{R}^{2d}$  of  $\pmb{F}$  corresponding to the first input token  $(< s >)$  as the aggregated representation.
comments.explanation: The notation $(< s >)$ appears to be a typo. In BERT/RoBERTa, the special classification token is typically denoted as [CLS] or <s>. The parentheses around <s> are unusual and may confuse readers. More importantly, Devlin et al. (2019) use the [CLS] token representation for classification, not necessarily the 'first input token'. The authors should clarify whether they're using the [CLS] token position or literally the first token position, as these are equivalent in BERT but the distinction matters for other architectures. Rewrite as 'corresponding to the [CLS] token (<s>)' to maintain consistency with standard notation.

comments.title: Spelling error in technical term
comments.quote: which demonstrates HIRE's dynamic ability to adapt for distinct tasks when computing the complimentary representation.
comments.explanation: The text contains a spelling error where 'complimentary' is used instead of 'complementary'. In the context of extracting information that complements the final layer output, the correct term is 'complementary'. This appears to be a typographical error that should be corrected for clarity and professionalism. Rewrite 'complimentary representation' as 'complementary representation' because the paper refers to extracting information that complements rather than provides compliments to the final layer.

comments.title: Grammar error in sentence structure
comments.quote: In the meantime, we observe also that even though there are subtle differences among these examples, they follow certain same patterns when calculating the complementary representation, for example, layers 21 and 22 contribute the most for almost all the examples and also the layers around them.
comments.explanation: The sentence contains grammatical errors that affect readability. 'In the meantime' is typically used for temporal transitions, not for presenting simultaneous observations. Additionally, 'certain same patterns' is awkward phrasing. The sentence structure would be clearer with 'Meanwhile' instead of 'In the meantime' and 'certain common patterns' instead of 'certain same patterns'. Rewrite 'In the meantime, we observe also that even though there are subtle differences among these examples, they follow certain same patterns when calculating the complementary representation' as 'Meanwhile, we also observe that despite subtle differences among these examples, they follow certain common patterns when calculating the complementary representation' because this improves grammatical correctness and clarity.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes HIRE, a method to fuse hidden representations from all layers of a pre-trained language model, claiming improvements on GLUE benchmarks. While the idea is intuitive, the paper suffers from several critical issues in methodology, evaluation, and framing that undermine its contributions.

The paper presents a straightforward extension of existing ideas (layer fusion) with a dynamic weighting mechanism. Its main strength is the empirical demonstration that fusing multiple layers can improve performance on some GLUE tasks. However, the evaluation is incomplete, the baselines are weak, and the claimed novelty is overstated given prior work.

Overview issues:

- Incomplete evaluation and missing statistical rigor: The paper reports results on GLUE but lacks statistical significance testing or confidence intervals. For a top-tier venue, this is a serious omission. The claim that improvements are 'entirely attributed' to HIRE is unsupported without proper controls. Additionally, the main results table (Table 1) is missing from the extracted text, making it impossible to verify the reported numbers. The authors should provide confidence intervals, perform statistical significance tests (e.g., bootstrap or paired t-tests), and ensure all tables are present and clearly formatted.

- Weak baselines and lack of comparison to state-of-the-art methods: The paper compares only against RoBERTa and a few other large models (MT-DNN, XLNet, ALBERT). It does not compare against other methods that modify PLM representations, such as adapter layers, prefix tuning, or other layer fusion techniques (e.g., FLAT, HyPe). Without these comparisons, it is unclear whether HIRE offers any advantage over existing approaches. The authors should include comparisons with at least 2-3 recent methods that also fuse or modify hidden representations.

- Overclaimed novelty and insufficient related work discussion: The paper claims to be 'the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model’s intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.' This is false, as prior work (e.g., ELMo, Tenney et al. 2019, Zhu et al. 2018) already explored layer fusion in fine-tuning scenarios. The paper's dynamic weighting mechanism is also similar to attention-based pooling used in many prior works. The authors should accurately position their contribution relative to existing methods and tone down novelty claims.

- Potential overfitting and lack of regularization analysis: The HIRE module adds 82 million parameters (from 355M to 437M) to RoBERTa. This is a substantial increase. The paper does not discuss whether the improvements come from the additional parameters rather than the fusion mechanism itself. The ablation study only removes components but does not control for parameter count. The authors should include a baseline that adds a similar number of parameters in a different way (e.g., extra layers in the PLM) to demonstrate that the improvement is not just due to increased capacity.

- Unclear training procedure and potential catastrophic forgetting: The paper does not specify whether the PLM backbone is fine-tuned jointly with HIRE or frozen. If the PLM is fine-tuned, there is a risk of catastrophic forgetting or unintended parameter interference. The training details (learning rates, number of epochs, etc.) are missing. The authors should clarify the training procedure, including whether the PLM is fine-tuned or frozen, and provide full hyperparameter settings.

- Limited analysis of layer selection and dynamic mechanism: The dynamic weighting mechanism is a core contribution, but the analysis in Section 4.2 is weak. The paper compares dynamic weights against fixed mean/random weights, but does not compare against a learned static weighting (e.g., learned per-layer scalars that are fixed across examples). This would isolate the benefit of per-example dynamic weighting. The authors should add an ablation with learned static weights to justify the dynamic mechanism.

- Missing reproducibility details: The paper does not provide code, specify PLM checkpoints, or report random seeds. For reproducibility, these are essential. The authors should release code, specify the exact RoBERTa checkpoint used, and report all random seeds.

- No simulation or controlled experiment isolating HIRE's effect: The paper claims HIRE extracts complementary information from hidden layers, but it never demonstrates this property directly. A standard approach in this field is to construct a synthetic dataset where the final layer representation is known to be missing certain information (e.g., a task that requires span-level features that only lower layers encode) and then show that HIRE recovers performance that the baseline RoBERTa cannot. The authors should design such a controlled experiment, perhaps using a probing task or a synthetic classification problem, to verify that HIRE actually captures complementary information rather than just adding capacity.

- No worked example or parametric case study: The paper presents no concrete, fully-specified example where the HIRE mechanism is computed and its behavior illustrated. For a theory paper, readers expect at least one small-scale example (e.g., a two-layer Transformer on a tiny vocabulary) where equations (2)-(5) are evaluated numerically and the resulting importance scores are shown. This would help calibrate intuition about how the dynamic weighting works and whether it behaves as claimed. The authors should add a simple synthetic example, perhaps with a 3-layer model on a binary classification task, and report the learned α_i values.

- Missing discussion of estimation and practical implementation challenges: The paper introduces a fusion network with a Bi-GRU that processes the concatenated matrix M of shape n×4d. For long sequences (e.g., n=512 in BERT), this GRU has to process a 4d-dimensional input per timestep, which is computationally heavy. The paper does not discuss training time, memory usage, or convergence issues compared to the baseline. Moreover, the HIRE module adds 82M parameters, but there is no analysis of whether this leads to overfitting on smaller GLUE tasks (e.g., RTE with only 2.5k examples). The authors should provide training time comparisons, discuss regularization strategies (e.g., dropout rates for the GRUs), and report performance variance across seeds for small datasets.

- No comparison to simpler parameter-matched baselines: The paper's ablation removes HIRE or the fusion network but does not control for the increase in parameters. A key question is whether the improvements come from the proposed mechanism or simply from having more parameters. The authors should add a baseline that increases RoBERTa's capacity by adding extra Transformer layers (or widening existing layers) to match the 437M parameter count of RoBERTa+HIRE. If this baseline matches or exceeds HIRE's performance, then the claimed benefit of the fusion mechanism is not supported. This is a standard control in NLP papers that propose new modules.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add statistical significance testing and confidence intervals for all main results.

- Compare against at least 2-3 state-of-the-art methods that also modify PLM representations (e.g., adapters, prefix tuning, FLAT).

- Include a parameter-matched baseline to show improvements are not solely due to increased capacity.

- Clarify the training procedure: whether the PLM is fine-tuned or frozen, and provide full hyperparameters.

- Add an ablation with learned static layer weights to justify the dynamic mechanism.

comments.title: Incorrect novelty claim about being first to use intermediate information in fine-tuning
comments.quote: To our best knowledge, our work is the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model’s intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: This claim is factually incorrect. Prior work such as Tenney et al. (2019) already explored ELMo-style scalar mixing of layer activations in fine-tuning scenarios for deep Transformer models (BERT and GPT), as the paper itself acknowledges earlier in the same paragraph. Additionally, adapter-based methods and other layer fusion techniques (e.g., FLAT, HyPe) predate this paper. The authors should remove or substantially soften this novelty claim and accurately position their contribution relative to existing fine-tuning approaches that use intermediate representations.

comments.title: Index error in Equation (5) summation bound
comments.quote: um_{i=0}^{l+1}s_{i}\bm{H}_{i}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d}$ (5)
comments.explanation: The summation in Equation (5) runs from i=0 to l+1, but the set of hidden states is defined as {H_0,...,H_l} (l+1 states total). The index l+1 is out of range. The correct upper bound should be l, yielding A = sum_{i=0}^l s_i H_i. This is a clear indexing error that would cause an off-by-one error in implementation. Rewrite the upper bound of the sum in Equation (5) from l+1 to l.

comments.title: Missing dev set table and incomplete data
comments.quote: Table 1 is missing from the markdown extraction for page 5.
comments.explanation: The dev set results (Table 1) are not present in the extracted text, making it impossible to verify the reported improvements (e.g., 1.7 points on CoLA, 0.4 on SST-2). Without the actual numbers, the claims are unverifiable. The authors must ensure that Table 1 is included in the submission with all task-specific scores for both RoBERTa and RoBERTa+HIRE, along with the baseline results from prior work. This is a basic requirement for a results section.

comments.title: Unsupported claim of full attribution to HIRE
comments.quote: It should be noted that the improvement is entirely attributed to the introduction of Hidden Representation Extractor and fusion network in our model.
comments.explanation: This claim is not supported by the evidence presented. The paper does not control for the increase in model parameters (from 355M to 437M) when comparing RoBERTa+HIRE to RoBERTa. Without a baseline that adds a similar number of parameters in a different way (e.g., extra Transformer layers), the improvement could be due to increased capacity rather than the proposed mechanism. The authors should either add such a control or soften the claim to state that the improvement is associated with the HIRE module, not entirely attributed to it.

comments.title: Missing statistical significance testing
comments.quote: We run our model with five different random seeds but the same hyperparameters and take the median value.
comments.explanation: Reporting only the median over five runs without confidence intervals or statistical significance tests is insufficient for a top-tier venue. The improvements reported (e.g., 0.4 points on SST-2, 0.3 points on QNLI) are small and could be within the noise of random seed variation. The authors should compute confidence intervals (e.g., bootstrap) or perform paired significance tests (e.g., approximate randomization) to demonstrate that the differences are statistically significant. Without this, readers cannot assess whether the claimed improvements are reliable.

comments.title: Inconsistent reporting of results on MRPC and STS-B
comments.quote: And on the MRPC, STS-B and RTE task, our model gets the same result as RoBERTa.
comments.explanation: This statement on the dev set contradicts the test set results reported later in the same section, where RoBERTa+HIRE outperforms RoBERTa on MRPC (0.7/0.9 points) and STS-B (0.2/0.1 points). The authors should reconcile this discrepancy: either the dev set results are truly identical (and the test set improvements come from multi-task initialization or ensembling), or the dev set numbers are slightly different but rounded to the same value. A clear explanation is needed, and the dev set table (Table 1) should be included to allow verification.

comments.title: Unclear training procedure for PLM backbone
comments.quote: following the original paper (Liu et al., 2019c), we fine-tune RoBERTa+HIRE separately for each of the GLUE tasks, using only task-specific training data.
comments.explanation: The paper does not specify whether the RoBERTa backbone is fine-tuned jointly with the HIRE module or frozen during HIRE training. If the backbone is fine-tuned, there is a risk of catastrophic forgetting or unintended parameter interference. If frozen, the improvement may be limited. The authors should explicitly state whether the PLM parameters are updated during fine-tuning, and if so, provide learning rates, number of epochs, and any regularization (e.g., dropout) used for the HIRE module to prevent overfitting, especially on small tasks like RTE (2.5k examples).

comments.title: Claim about HIRE being crucial is not supported by relative drops
comments.quote: As can be seen from the table, to use of HIRE to extract the hidden information is crucial to the model: Matthews correlation of CoLA and Pearson correlation coefficient of STS-B drop dramatically by 1.2 and 0.9 points if it's removed. We also observe that fusion network is an important component that contributes  $1.5 / 0.2$  gains on the CoLA and STS-B tasks.
comments.explanation: The text claims HIRE is 'crucial' based on drops of 1.2 (CoLA) and 0.9 (STS-B) when removed. However, removing the fusion network causes a larger drop on CoLA (1.5) and a smaller drop on STS-B (0.2). The relative importance is not uniform across tasks: on CoLA, the fusion network contributes more than HIRE. The authors should either qualify the claim (e.g., 'HIRE is crucial, especially on STS-B') or provide a rationale for why the fusion network's contribution is larger on CoLA. Rewrite the sentence to reflect the task-dependent importance, e.g., 'HIRE contributes substantially on both tasks, while the fusion network provides a larger gain on CoLA (1.5) than on STS-B (0.2).'

comments.title: Missing justification for self-fusion design in ablation
comments.explanation: The ablation replaces HIRE's output with a copy of R, but the design choice of using [R; R; R+R; R∘R] is not justified. This specific concatenation may not be a fair baseline because it introduces artificial redundancy (R appears twice) and uses R+R = 2R, which is just a scaled version of R. A simpler baseline would be to pass R directly to the output layer without any fusion, or to use [R; R] only. The authors should explain why this particular self-fusion is appropriate, or use a more neutral baseline (e.g., just R). Add a justification or replace with a simpler baseline.

comments.title: Scope gap: ablation only on two tasks, claim about general importance
comments.quote: The results are aligned with our assumption that HIRE extracts the complementary information from the hidden layers while the fusion network fuses it with the preliminary representation.
comments.explanation: The ablation study is conducted only on CoLA and STS-B, yet the conclusion is stated as a general property of the model. The paper does not claim that these two tasks are representative of all NLP tasks, but the phrasing 'aligned with our assumption' implies a general mechanism. To avoid overclaiming, the authors should either add experiments on more diverse tasks (e.g., classification, QA) or explicitly limit the claim to the tasks tested. Add a caveat: 'On CoLA and STS-B, these results support our assumption...'

comments.title: Table 4 column header mismatch
comments.quote:
|  Method | Mc |   |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  HIRE (dynamic) | 69.7 |   |
|  mean, all 25 layers | 68.9 | (-0.8)  |
|  mean, last 6 layers | 68.0 | (-1.7)  |
|  mean, first 6 layers | 68.8 | (-0.9)  |
|  random, all 25 layers | 69.3 | (-0.4)  |
|  random, last 6 layers | 68.1 | (-1.6)  |
comments.explanation: The table caption says the results are for both CoLA and STS-B, but the table only shows a single column of numbers labeled 'Mc' (Matthews correlation, which is CoLA's metric). STS-B uses Pearson/Spearman correlation, not Mc. The table is missing the STS-B column entirely. This is either a caption error or a missing column. Rewrite the caption to say 'on the development set of CoLA' (matching Table 5 and Table 6) and remove the reference to STS-B, or add the STS-B results as a second column.

comments.title: Table 5 is referenced but not present
comments.quote: Table 5 compares our proposed mechanism
comments.explanation: The text says 'Table 5 compares our proposed mechanism with diverse fixed combinations...' but the actual Table 5 shown in the extracted text is a 2-row table with columns 'Number' 0-3 and 'Mc' values 68.5, 69.3, 69.7, 68.5. That table does not contain any fixed-combination baselines (mean/random over layer ranges). The table that actually contains those comparisons is Table 4. The reference to Table 5 is a cross-reference error — it should point to Table 4. Rewrite 'Table 5' as 'Table 4' in the sentence following the page break.

comments.title: Table 6 caption mislabels content
comments.quote: Table 6: Ablation study over GRU layer number in fusion network on the development set of CoLA. The results are a median Matthews correlation of five random runs. The best result is in bold.
comments.explanation: The caption says the table studies 'GRU layer number' but the table only shows a single row of Mc values indexed by 'Number' 0-3. There is no column indicating what each number means (e.g., number of GRU layers). The table is also placed in the middle of a paragraph about fixed importance scores, not about GRU depth. This makes the table's purpose and interpretation unclear. Add a column or row label explicitly stating what 'Number' refers to (e.g., 'Number of GRU layers') and move the table to the subsection where GRU depth is discussed, or remove it if it is not discussed.

comments.title: Conclusion overstates evidence from single dataset
comments.quote: The results enable us to conclude that the hidden-state weighting mechanism introduced by HIRE may indeed adapt to diverse downstream tasks for better performance.
comments.explanation: The conclusion that the mechanism 'adapts to diverse downstream tasks' is based on results from a single task (CoLA). A single dataset cannot demonstrate diversity of tasks. The paper should either (a) present similar ablation studies on at least one other task (e.g., STS-B, which is mentioned in the Table 4 caption) or (b) temper the claim to say 'on CoLA' rather than 'diverse downstream tasks.' As written, the claim is unsupported by the evidence shown.

comments.title: Single dataset and metric insufficient for general claim about GRU layers
comments.quote: To investigate the effect of GRU layer number in the fusion network, we set the GRU layer number from 0 to 3 and conduct the ablation experiments on the development dataset of CoLA. Table 6 shows the results. We observe that the modest number 2 would be a better choice.
comments.explanation: The claim that 'the modest number 2 would be a better choice' is based solely on results from the CoLA development set. CoLA is a single-task benchmark (grammatical acceptability) and may not be representative of other tasks (e.g., NER, sentiment analysis, question answering). The paper's own stated scope includes multiple GLUE tasks and NER, so a hyperparameter choice (GRU layers) that is optimal for CoLA may not generalize. The authors should either (a) report the ablation on at least two diverse tasks (e.g., CoLA and RTE or CoLA and MRPC) to support the claim, or (b) explicitly limit the claim to CoLA and note that task-specific tuning may be needed. Rewrite the quoted text as: 'To investigate the effect of GRU layer number in the fusion network, we set the GRU layer number from 0 to 3 and conduct ablation experiments on the development datasets of CoLA and RTE. Tables 6 and 7 show the results. We observe that 2 layers is a reasonable choice across these two tasks, though task-specific tuning may yield different optimal values.'

comments.title: Missing figure captions and incomplete analysis
comments.quote: Figure 2: Left: Distribution of importance scores over different layers when computing the complementary representation for various NLU tasks. The numbers on the abscissa axis indicate the corresponding layer with 0 being the first layer and 24 being the last layer. Right: Distribution of importance scores over different layers for each example of SST-2 dataset. The number on the ordinate axis denotes the index of the example.
comments.explanation: The figure caption refers to 'the complementary representation' but the text in the analysis uses 'complimentary representation' (a typo). More importantly, the analysis claims that 'the most important contribution occurs below the final layer for all the tasks except STS-B and RTE' but does not provide any numerical threshold or quantitative evidence for what constitutes 'most important.' The heatmap is not visible in the extracted text, so the reader cannot verify the claim. The authors should either include the actual heatmap values (e.g., a table of average importance scores per layer per task) or define a clear criterion (e.g., 'layers with importance score > 0.1') to support the qualitative observation. Without this, the analysis remains subjective and unverifiable.

comments.title: Contradiction in importance score distribution for SST-2
comments.quote: It shows that HIRE can adapt not only distinct tasks but also the different examples. In the meantime, we observe also that even though there are subtle differences among these examples, they follow certain same patterns when calculating the complementary representation, for example, layers 21 and 22 contribute the most for almost all the examples and also the layers around them.
